coredata.dc:title: High quantum yield AIE covalent organic frameworks for sensitive pH monitoring, and copper toxicosis Diagnosis&Remission

Wang, Dandan
Yang, Yulian
Zhu, Yunfei

Wu, Jianming
coredata.dc:description: The luminescence properties of covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have attracted significant attention for biomedical applications. However, conventional fluorescent COFs often suffer from weak fluorescence due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), limiting their utility. Given the critical need for highly efficient and sensitive biosensing tools, herein, we propose an aggregation-induced emission (AIE) COF, named COF-Bpy, with the quantum yield up to 25%. Benefiting from the active nitrogen sites within the bipyridine unit, COF-Bpy demonstrates remarkable pH monitoring capabilities across a wide pH range and displays strong coordination affinity towards Cu2+, achieving satisfactory detection and efficient adsorption of Cu2+. These properties make COF-Bpy superior in cellular pH imaging, and diagnosis&remission of copper toxicosis at both cellular and nematode levels, as it reduces the late apoptotic cells, improves nematode survival and locomotor states. Additionally, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of dynamic quenching with metal-to-ligand charge transfer is clarified. Briefly, this study not only presents a novel strategy for fabrication of AIE-COFs, but also provides a promising avenue for early diagnosis of pH-related diseases, and offers a theranostics for copper toxicosis management.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic frameworks
Aggregation-induced emission
pH monitoring
Copper toxicosis
Theranostics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro Cu2+ detection and adsorption
Selectivity, sensitivity and stability of Cu2+ detection
Fluorescence analysis of Cu2+ in actual samples
Application of theranostics for copper toxicosis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan (Grant No. 23NSFSC5092), a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2024LZXNYDJ018), a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040), a project of Intelligent Policing Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (ZNJW2024KFMS005), the Cooperation Project of Xuzhou District Government and Southwest Medical University (2021XZXNYD01), Science and Technology Planning Project of Yibin City, China [Grant Nos. 2022NY020, 2021ZYY009 and 2021ZYY005].

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22474114), the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan (Grant Nos. 23NSFSC5092 and 2024NSFSC0610), a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2024LZXNYDJ018), a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040), a project of Intelligent Policing Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (ZNJW2024KFMS005), the Cooperation Project of Xuzhou District Government and Southwest Medical University (2021XZXNYD01), Science and Technology Planning Project of Yibin City, China [Grant Nos. 2022NY020, 2021ZYY009 and 2021ZYY005].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra of precursors and COF-Bpy. (B) Solid-state 13C NMR spectra of COF-Bpy. (C) XPS survey spectra of COF-Bpy. (D) C 1s region of COF-Bpy. (E) N 1s region of COF-Bpy. (F) O 1s region of COF-Bpy. (G) SEM image of COF-Bpy. (H) TEM image of COF-Bpy. (I) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the COF-Bpy. (J) TGA analysis of COF-Bpy. (K) Water contact angle changes of COF-Bpy within 10 s. (L) Solvent-induced discoloration behavior of COF-Bpy in different solvents (100 μg mL−1).

(A) QY of COF-DHB and COF-Bpy. (B) Fluorescence spectra of COF-DHB in DMSO/THF system. Inset: UV images of COF-DHB at different f
D . (C) Fluorescence spectra of COF-Bpy in DMSO/THF system. Inset: UV images of COF-Bpy at different f
D . (D) Fluorescence emission wavelength of COF-Bpy at different f
D . (E) Relative emission intensity plots of COF-Bpy and COF-DHB at different f
D (RSD = 2.3%). (F) Fluorescent lifetime of COF-Bpy at 0% and 90% f
D . (G) Fluorescence spectra of COF-DHB in H2O/THF system. Inset: UV images of COF-DHB at different f
w . (H) Fluorescence spectra of COF-Bpy in H2O/THF system. Inset: UV images of COF-Bpy at different f
w . (I) Fluorescence emission wavelength of COF-Bpy at different f
w . (J) Relative emission intensity plots of COF-Bpy and COF-DHB at different f
w (RSD = 2.4%). (K–M) DLS results of the particle size distribution of COF-Bpy at 60%, 80% and 99% f
w . (N–P) SEM images of COF-Bpy at 60%, 80% and 99% f
w . (Q) UV–vis spectra of COF-Bpy at 0% and 99% f
D . (R) UV–vis spectra of COF-Bpy at 0% and 50% f
w . (S) Molecular orbital calculations and energy gap comparisons between COF-DHB and COF-Bpy. Error bars = SD (n = 3).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of COF-Bpy at different pH. Inset: UV images of COF-Bpy at different pH. (B) Linear relationship between FX/F6.8 and pH, the subscript means pH value. Inset: fluorescence titration plots of COF-Bpy. (C) ICT of COF-Bpy. (D) Zeta potential of COF-Bpy. (E) Electrostatic potential distribution of COF-Bpy. (F) Protonation of COF-Bpy. (G) Reversible pH response of COF-Bpy. (H) PXRD spectra of COF-Bpy before and after five cycles of reversible pH response. (I) Relative fluorescence intensity of COF-Bpy with different ions. (RSD = 10%) (J) Fluorescence spectra of COF-Bpy with different concentrations of Cu2+. (K) Linear correlation between COF-Bpy and Cu2+. (L) Fluorescent lifetime of COF-Bpy before and after adding Cu2+. (M) Stern-Volmer plots at different temperatures. (N) UV–vis spectra of Cu2+, COF-Bpy and mixture. (O) Molecular orbital calculations of COF-Bpy and [COF-Bpy + Cu2+]. Error bars = SD (n = 3).

(A) Inverted fluorescence imaging of HCCC cells at different pH. (B) Linear relationship between imaging intensity and pH. Inset: imaging intensity statistics. (C) Inverted fluorescence imaging of HCCC cells and (D) Statistics of imaging intensity at different concentrations of Cu2+. (E) Relative imaging intensity at range of 10 nM-10 μM of Cu2+. (F–H) Linear correlation at range of 10 nM–100 nM, 0.1 μM–1.0 μM and 1 μM–10 μM of Cu2+. (I) Remission effect of COF-Bpy on copper toxicosis cells. (J) Statistics of apoptosis cells under different treatments. (K–N) Dot plot of HCCC cells apoptosis with different treatments measured by flow cytometry. Statistical significance: nsP > 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, and ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001. Scale bar = 100 μm. Error bars = SD (n = 3).

(A) Detection of Cu2+ in nematodes by fluorescence images. (B) Linear relationship between imaging intensity and Cu2+ concentration. (C) Statistics of nematode survival. Improvement of (D–F) body bends, (G–I) average speed and (J–L) maximum speed of nematodes with COF-Bpy treatment. (M) Recording of motion trajectories of nematodes. Statistical significance: nsP > 0.05, ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, ∗∗∗P < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001. Scale bar = 100 μm.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis and application of COF-Bpy.

head.ce:title: High quantum yield AIE covalent organic frameworks for sensitive pH monitoring, and copper toxicosis Diagnosis&Remission

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The luminescence properties of covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have attracted significant attention for biomedical applications. However, conventional fluorescent COFs often suffer from weak fluorescence due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), limiting their utility. Given the critical need for highly efficient and sensitive biosensing tools, herein, we propose an aggregation-induced emission (AIE) COF, named COF-Bpy, with the quantum yield up to 25%. Benefiting from the active nitrogen sites within the bipyridine unit, COF-Bpy demonstrates remarkable pH monitoring capabilities across a wide pH range and displays strong coordination affinity towards Cu2+, achieving satisfactory detection and efficient adsorption of Cu2+. These properties make COF-Bpy superior in cellular pH imaging, and diagnosis&remission of copper toxicosis at both cellular and nematode levels, as it reduces the late apoptotic cells, improves nematode survival and locomotor states. Additionally, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of dynamic quenching with metal-to-ligand charge transfer is clarified. Briefly, this study not only presents a novel strategy for fabrication of AIE-COFs, but also provides a promising avenue for early diagnosis of pH-related diseases, and offers a theranostics for copper toxicosis management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are a versatile class of porous crystalline materials formed via covalent bonding between organic building blocks. Due to their high surface area, rich functional groups and customizable structures, COFs have emerged as potential candidates in fields like sensing (Zhang et al., 2024), adsorption (Li et al., 2024), electron conduction (Yang et al., 2021), energy storage (Lv et al., 2018) and catalysis (Qian and Jiang, 2024). Recently, research has focused on fluorescent COFs for sensing and adsorption applications (Jiang et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). These dual-function COFs have shown significant advancements, offering promising prospects for clinical theranostics (Liang et al., 2023).

However, existing fluorescent COFs face challenges that hinder their suitability for advanced dual-function platforms. The conventional imine-linked COFs, which are typically synthesized via Schiff base reactions between aldehyde and amino precursors, often suffer from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) due to strong π-π interactions between COF layers (Dalapati et al., 2016). This limitation significantly compromises the performance of dual-functional sensing and adsorption platforms, especially in detecting and enriching target analytes within biological systems. Addressing these shortcomings is essential to realize the full potential of fluorescent COFs in clinical theranostics.

Introducing the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect into COFs is a convenient and efficient strategy to enhance their optical performance. It offers various advantages, including high quantum yield (QY), strong fluorescence emission, excellent light stability, low background signal, self-quenching inhibition, biocompatibility and positive concentration correlation (Xu and Cheng, 2024). These properties render AIE materials valuable for biological imaging (Gong et al., 2023), disease diagnosis and treatment (Fan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022), and target-specific monitoring (Di et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2024). Importantly, these advantages of AIE help overcome the limitations of existing ACQ fluorescent COFs. To date, several AIE fluorescent imine-linked COFs, such as COF-919 (Zhang L. et al., 2023b), TDTA-COF, BTDTA-COF, BTDBETA-COF (Zhang L. et al., 2023a) and HACOF (Xia et al., 2023), have been developed, demonstrating promising strategies for constructing AIE effect to enhance optical properties. Inspired by these studies, to obtain the AIE-COFs, it is imperative to destruct π-π interlayer accumulation (DIA), interfere with molecular layer planarization (IMLP) and restrict intramolecular rotation (RIR) of COFs.

In this work, we synthesized a novel AIE-COF with C2+C2 topological structure (named COF-Bpy), with 2,2′-bipyridine-5,5′-diamineas (Bpy) as the precursor. Its mutually perpendicular skeletons and distorted conformations promote DIA, IMLP and RIR simultaneously, enhancing its AIE effect and fluorescence properties, leading to strong green fluorescence and satisfactory fluorescence QY. Furthermore, the active nitrogen site in the bipyridine unit endows COF-Bpy with pH monitoring and metal ion coordination capabilities, thereby expanding its potential applications in pH and Cu2+ sensing, as well as copper toxicosis remediation through adsorption. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of COF-Bpy is discussed in detail, using the characterization of fluorescence lifetimes, Stern-Volmer plots and UV–vis spectra, supplemented by Density Functional Theory (DFT) simulation calculations. Detailed synthesis and application protocols of COF-Bpy are shown in Scheme 1 .
Detailed experimental procedures are in the Supporting Information .

The crystal structure of COFs plays a pivotal role in their synthesis and application. Solvothermal method can produce highly crystalline COFs, but it often entails high temperatures and generates glass waste, contradicting with green chemistry and sustainable development. To address this, we optimized an energy efficient room temperature synthesis strategy, including solvent choice and volume, catalyst dosage, and reaction time to enhance the crystallinity of COF-Bpy.

PXRD was utilized to assess the crystallinity of COF-Bpy. Given solvent polarity and composition are critical factors for optimal framework growth and crystallization (Elewa et al., 2022), COF-Bpy synthesized in acetonitrile, acetone, dioxane, N,N-dimethylformamide, mesitylene/dioxane and n-BuOH/o-DCB were tested. Among them, acetone was proven the most effective (Fig. S1A). Since the synthesis of COFs is a reversible "aging" process, suitable reaction conditions are required for continuous "self-healing" and "error adjustment" (Huang et al., 2020). Thus, reacting in 9.0 mL of acetone with 0.4 mL of HAc for 72 h facilitated COF-Bpy crystallization (Figs. S1B–S1D), with 84% yield. PXRD simulation experiment using Materials Studio confirmed the hexagonal space group (P3) conformation (Rwp = 2.70%, Rp = 2.03%) of COF-Bpy crystal structure. The AB-stacking mode with a 4.59 Å layer spacing matched experimental data better than AA-stacking (Figs. S1E–S1G).

To elucidate chemical structure and composition of COF-Bpy, FTIR, XPS and Solid-state 13C NMR spectra were analyzed. FTIR spectra (Fig. 1 A) show that the aldehyde C=O (1720 cm−1) of DFP and N-H (3331-3199 cm−1) of Bpy disappear, and C=N (1629 cm−1) of COF-Bpy appears. And the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 1B) displays a characteristic peak of -C∗ = N- at 155.2 ppm. XPS analysis (Fig. 1C) indicates the elemental percentages of C (77.3%), N (16.5%) and O (6.14%). The C 1s region (Fig. 1D) shows peaks for pyridine C (284.6 eV), C-C (284.0 eV), C=N (285.4 eV), and C-N (287.2 eV). The N 1s region (Fig. 1E) includes peaks for pyridine N (398.5 eV), N=C (399.0 eV), and N-C (399.4 eV). The O 1s region (Fig. 1F) displays peaks for O=C (531.6 eV), atmospheric oxygen (533.3 eV) and residual moisture (531.9 eV). These results further illustrate that the bonding of imine linkage, confirming successful synthesis of COF-Bpy.

SEM and TEM (Fig. 1G-1H) were employed to reveal the microstructure of COF-Bpy with granular-textured stacked flakes and lamellar-stacked sheet crystal morphology. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms analysis (Fig. 1I) indicates a specific surface area of 107 m2 g−1. TGA (Fig. 1J) shows that initial weight loss about 14% at 350 °C due to residual solvent and moisture evaporation, and further weight loss about 29% until 900 °C, due to COF-Bpy carbon skeleton degradation, demonstrating the thermal stability of COF-Bpy. The water contact angle (Fig. 1K) of COF-Bpy decreases from 54° to 47° within 10 s, indicating its hydrophilic properties, which means it is suitable for aqueous applications. These results underscore the successful synthesis and comprehensive characterization of COF-Bpy, exhibiting its potential for applications of sensing, adsorption and catalysis.

Conventional COFs often exhibit a pronounced ACQ effect due to π-π stacking, resulting in weak fluorescence performance. To address this limitation, it is viable to introduce AIE effect, with key strategies of DIA, IMLP, and RIR. Interestingly, COFs inherently possess a viscous microenvironment that facilitates RIR (Xia et al., 2023). Therefore, screening AIEgens precursors with weak π-π interactions and non-planar molecular layers is essential for achieving AIE effect, improving optical properties of COFs. Although polymeric molecular probes containing AIEgens have been reported, they lack the ordered stacked structure (Shustova et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2023). Thus, synthesizing orderly crystallized, porous and highly emissive AIE-COFs is crucial for advancing fluorescent COFs with superior optical properties.

Chromophores containing freely rotating nitrogen-heterocyclic fragments, such as bipyridine, show significant AIE effect (Jiang et al., 2023; Starnovskaya et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023). In the dispersed state, bipyridine rotates freely, facilitating non-radiative transitions. While in the aggregated state, this rotation is restricted, promoting radiative decay, leading to AIE effect (Asad et al., 2022). Inspired by these works, Bpy was employed as a luminescent precursor to synthesize highly emissive fluorescent COF-Bpy, with QY reaching up to 25%, which even surpasses some reported fluorescent COFs by 165 times (Table S1).

Given the unique photophysical phenomena of chromophores Bpy, it is necessary to explore the fluorescence properties of COF-Bpy in different solvents. COF-Bpy was dispersed in various hydrophilic solvents and aprotic solvents, differences in the dispersibility, fluorescence color and fluorescent intensity can be observed (Fig. 1L). Remarkably, COF-Bpy exhibits the highest fluorescence intensity in DMSO. Subsequently, fluorescence spectra of COF-Bpy in different solvents were recorded at excitation wavelength of 330 nm, except for H2O at 390 nm. According to the order of hydrophilic solvents polarity, the emission wavelength of COF-Bpy is red-shifted with the solvent polarity increasing (Fig. S2A). It also can be observed in aprotic solvents that, COF-Bpy has the largest emission wavelength in DMSO compared to others (Fig. S2B). Since the fluorescence intensity of COF-Bpy (DMSO) is the most prominent, and considering the universal applicability in water system, COF-Bpy was further studied in these two solvents separately.

To investigate fluorescence properties, a COF-Bpy analog, COF-DHB, was synthesized as model compound for subsequent research. Synthesis details are in Table S2, and crystallinity optimizations are shown in Figs. S3A–S3D. FTIR spectra (Fig. S3E) shows C=N peak appears at 1622 cm−1, which confirms COF-DHB was synthesized successfully. Fluorescence spectra of COF-Bpy (Figs. S4A–S4D) show the optimal excitation at 400 nm in DMSO and 390 nm in H2O, with UV absorption peaks around 300–400 nm. While COF-DHB has the optimal excitation at 450 nm, with a broad UV absorption peak around 350–600 nm (Figs. S4E–S4F). Interestingly, QY of COF-Bpy (25%) is much higher than that of COF-DHB (0.18%) (Fig. 2 A, S5A-S5B).

Fluorescence spectra of COF-Bpy in DMSO/THF and H2O/THF systems were measured to interpret its AIE effect. In DMSO/THF system, COF-DHB shows negligible emission (Fig. 2B), while COF-Bpy almost has no emission in pure THF, it presents pronounced fluorescence enhancement and redshift with the DMSO fraction (f
D ) increasing (Fig. 2C-2D). The αAIE value was calculated as 223 (Fig. 2E). Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of COF-Bpy at 90% f
D (3.70 ns) is much longer than that at 0% f
D (1.26 ns) (Fig. 2F), indicating the formation of molecular aggregates (Kang et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2014; Starnovskaya et al., 2023). In H2O/THF system, COF-DHB still shows no emission (Fig. 2G), while emission intensity of COF-Bpy peaks at 50% H2O fraction (f
w ), with a 20 nm redshift and the αAIE value of about 147 (Fig. 2H-2J). This redshift may associate with solvent polarity, indicating the presence of Twisted Intramolecular Charge Transfer (TICT) effect (Han et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2014). However, increasing f
w (>50%) further reduces the emission intensity (Fig. 2J). Particle sizes and micromorphology of COF-Bpy at different f
w (60%, 80% and 99%) were analyzed. With f
w increasing, the particle sizes increase from 684.8 nm to 1666 nm (Fig. 2K-2M), and the aggregates change from tightly packed to loosely flattened (Fig. 2N-2P), which weaken AIE effect, leading to emission reduction. UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2Q-2R) confirm aggregates formation, based on the peak shift and an additional peak at 280 nm caused by light scattering from aggregates (Starnovskaya et al., 2023). Simulations elucidate planar six-membered ring structure of COF-DHB (Figs. S6A–S6D), stabilized by hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking, causing ACQ effect (Zhang E.S. et al., 2023). In contrast, COF-Bpy has mutually perpendicular skeleton and chair-like non-planar conformation (Figs. S6E–S6H), which promotes DIA, IMLP and RIR, causing AIE effect. DFT calculations confirm the smaller energy gap of COF-Bpy (Fig. 2S), which means energy for electron exciting from Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) is smaller, facilitating charge transfer (George et al., 2023) and resulting in higher reactivity and superior optoelectronic performance (Juma et al., 2019; Lai et al., 2019). These findings confirm the AIE effect of COF-Bpy, highlighting its superior optical properties and potential for related applications.

Given the bipyridyl nitrogen of COF-Bpy can serve as the protonation site, it is hypothesized that pH fluctuation can be indicated by COF-Bpy-based fluorescence analysis. Fluorescence spectra measured at various pH levels (Figures 3 A-3B) illustrate that the fluorescence intensity decreases with pH increasing, with a linear correlation (R2 = 0.9904) in pH range of 2.0–14. The fluorescence titration curve (Fig. 3B), derived from the formula provided in Supporting Information , estimates pKa value of 6.2 for COF-Bpy. Due to its suitable pKa and sensitivity in a wide range of pH, COF-Bpy is promising as a sensor for monitoring pH fluctuation in vivo.

COF-Bpy is a nitrogen-rich structure, with bipyridine, pyridine and imine. The lone pair electrons in the pyridyl and bipyridyl groups contribute to aromaticity, they are less available for electron donation, so Intramolecular Charge Transfer (ICT) mainly occurs through the imine nitrogen, transferring charge to the pyridyl and bipyridyl groups (Fig. 3C). The Zeta potential of COF-Bpy (Fig. 3D) shows the decrease from 42.0 mV to −30.8 mV with pH increasing, indicating the pH responses are attributed to protonation and deprotonation (Meng et al., 2021). Electrostatic potential distribution calculations (Fig. 3E) reveal that bipyridine nitrogen has higher electronegativity than pyridine and imine nitrogen, suggesting that H+ may predominantly affect fluorescence emission by coordinating with bipyridine nitrogen. To validate this hypothesis, COF-BZD (lacking bipyridine nitrogen) was synthesized as a control model compound. Detailed synthesis conditions are in Table S2 and crystallinity optimizations are shown in Figs. S7A–S7D. FTIR spectra (Fig. S7E) confirm successful synthesis of COF-BZD, due to the C=N peak appears at 1627 cm−1. Fluorescence spectra of COF-BZD measured at varying pH (Figs. S8A–S8B) reveal that the fluorescence spectra and intensity remain largely unaffected by pH. These results confirm that bipyridine nitrogen of COF-Bpy can readily accept protons (Fig. 3F), facilitating the ICT effect (Shi et al., 2020), further increasing the fluorescence intensity. Conversely, deprotonation reduces fluorescence intensity due to the inhibited ICT effect. Thus, COF-Bpy exhibits sensitive pH responsiveness.

Given the COF-Bpy exhibits a reversible "On-Off" fluorescence response through protonation and deprotonation, its pH cycling detectability was investigated. Interestingly, after three cycles of reversible pH response, the fluorescence intensity difference between pH 2.0 and pH 13 increases with the number of cycles (Fig. 3G), which may indicate that the cycling detectability of COF-Bpy should be improved. We interpreted the influence of this process on the stability of COF-Bpy structure. Remarkably, the crystallinity of COF-Bpy decreases with pH cycle number, the crystal characteristic peaks completely disappear until the fifth cycle (Fig. 3H). However, negligible change is shown in the FTIR spectra of COF-Bpy after five cycles, especially the C=N peak at 1625 cm−1 (Fig. S8C). These results demonstrate that the cycling detectability of COF-Bpy may relate to crystallinity rather than chemical bonding. During extreme pH conditions cycling, COF-Bpy continuously associated and dissociated with H+, making its crystal structure change. With the cycling number increasing, the crystal form of COF-Bpy was destroyed completely, thus the difference in fluorescence intensity became larger.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Cu2+ detection and adsorption
Selectivity, sensitivity and stability of Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Given the abundance of ionic coordination sites of COF-Bpy, we explored its fluorescence responses to various ions. Remarkably, Cu2+ significantly quenches fluorescence of COF-Bpy, while other ions have minimal effects (Fig. 3I). The fluorescence intensity decreases with Cu2+ concentration, showing a robust linear relationship in the range of 10 pM-1.0 μM, with LOD of 3.3 pM (Fig. 3J-3K). The intra-day relative standard deviations (RSDs) range from 0.52% to 1.7%, and inter-day RSDs range from 1.5% to 8.7% (Table S3).

Furthermore, the fluorescence stability of COF-Bpy was evaluated, encompassing time stability and salt interference stability. The fluorescence of COF-Bpy remains stable for up to 180 min (Fig. S8D), and high concentrations of NaCl and Na2SO4 have negligible effects on Cu2+ detection sensitivity (Figs. S8E–S8F). These results confirm that COF-Bpy has good sensitivity, stability and applicability for detecting trace Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of Cu2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, industrialization, urbanization and agricultural activities have increased copper release into the environment. Copper accumulates in ecosystems, followed by bioaccumulation, posing significant health risks. To demonstrate the efficient applicability of COF-Bpy to Cu2+ detection, a fluorescence-based method was developed to detect Cu2+ in three environmental and two biological samples. The reliability was assessed via the standard addition method in local pond water, river water and soil, as well as balsa fish and river shrimp sourced from local markets. In environmental samples, the measured recoveries were 95.6%–108%, with RSDs of 1.2%–8.6%, while in biological samples, the recoveries were 95.7%–114%, with RSDs of 1.9%–7.8% (Tables S5–S6). These indicate that COF-Bpy-based fluorescence detection method offers accurate recovery rates, satisfactory RSDs and high sensitivity, underscoring its potential for practical application in environment and organism.

To assess adsorption capacity of COF-Bpy for Cu2+, the adsorption isotherm was conducted. The adsorption capacity of COF-Bpy increases with Cu2+ concentration, reaching 15.8 mg g−1 (Fig. S9A). Additionally, the adsorption kinetic was studied (Fig. S9B), indicating rapid adsorption from 0 to 10 min, until equilibrium at 40 min. These are attributed to the porous structure of COF-Bpy provides numerous adsorption sites for Cu2+. Once the sites were saturated, the adsorption stabilized. These findings highlight the potential of COF-Bpy for Cu2+ enrichment. Further discussions of adsorption models are in Supporting Information .

Given its superior fluorescence properties, COF-Bpy is promising for fluorescence imaging. Cytotoxicity was assessed using the MTT assay. It exhibits no significant cytotoxic effects on HCCC, HeLa and RAW264.7 cells within 0–400 μg mL−1, and on MCF7 cells within 200 μg mL−1 (Figures S10A-S10D). Additionally, HCCC were treated with COF-Bpy, and observed over 10 days. After 10 days, the cell morphology is normal and fluorescence imaging is still clear (Fig. S10E), indicating low toxicity and sustained fluorescence of COF-Bpy, making it suitable for subsequent cell imaging.

To enhance the efficacy of COF-Bpy for imaging, optimal imaging concentration and time were investigated. The fluorescence intensity in HCCC increases with COF-Bpy concentration (Figs. S11A–S11B) and imaging durations (Figs. S11C–S11D). Considering the potential cytotoxicity at higher concentrations and ensuring stable imaging results, 200 μg mL−1 of COF-Bpy imaging for 30 min was identified as the optimal imaging conditions.

Intracellular pH level plays a crucial role in cell functions such as proliferation, apoptosis, and enzyme activity. Its abnormal fluctuations can lead to cellular dysfunction and various diseases. Therefore, monitoring intracellular pH is crucial in medical research. COF-Bpy, with its broad pH monitoring capabilities, was employed to image and monitor pH levels in HCCC. The fluorescence intensity of HCCC decreases with pH increasing from 2.0 to 12, displaying a negatively correlated linear relationship (y = −4.436x + 76.36, R2 = 0.9935) (Figures 4 A-4B). These results confirm the impressive pH biosensing potential of COF-Bpy for cell.

The cellular uptake mechanism of COF-Bpy was investigated. The energy dependence of this process was examined by pre-treating HCCC with 4 °C and NaN3 to disrupt ATP production and block the endocytic pathway. After adding COF-Bpy for 30 min, fluorescence intensity is significantly reduced in NaN3-treated cells and nearly absent in 4 °C-treated cells, indicating energy-dependent endocytosis (Figs. S12A–S12B). To determine the specific endocytic pathway, four inhibitors were employed, including Amiloride (micropinocytosis), Genistein (caveola-mediated endocytosis), MβCD (lipid raft-mediated endocytosis), and CPZ (clathrin-mediated endocytosis). The fluorescence intensity reduces by 50% after Amiloride and Genistein treatment, while treated with MβCD and CPZ, it shows similar intensity to the Control (Figs. S12A–S12B), revealing that cellular uptake of COF-Bpy involves energy consumption and primarily occurs via micropinocytosis and caveola-mediated endocytosis (Fig. S12C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of theranostics for copper toxicosis
ce:section.ce:para: Since the ability of COF-Bpy to simultaneously detect and adsorb Cu2+, it is necessary to further investigate its performance on living cells and biological individuals (represented by nematodes), to evaluate the potential application of the COF-Bpy-based theranostics for copper toxicosis.

The potential of COF-Bpy for intracellular Cu2+ detection was first investigated. The fluorescence intensity of cells decreases with concentration of Cu2+, displaying piecewise linear correlations within the corresponding range (Fig. 4C-4E), the detailed linear equations and R2 are shown in Fig. 4F-4H. It is worth mentioning that the COF-Bpy-based imaging method enables the precise detection of Cu2+ at nanomolar scale.

To assess the ability of COF-Bpy to adsorb Cu2+ and alleviate copper toxicosis of cells, we evaluated cell viability using MTT assay. Cu group (copper-poisoned cells) has significantly lower viability compared to the Control group, while COF-Bpy treatment (100–300 μg mL−1) can markedly improve cell viability of copper-poisoned cells (Fig. 4I). Subsequently, Annexin V-FITC/PI flow cytometry was used to assess apoptosis. With COF-Bpy treatment (200 μg mL−1) alone for 24 h, minimal increase in total apoptotic cells, indicating its good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity (Fig. 4J-4L, Table S7). In contrast, exposure to Cu2+ (1 mM) for 24 h strikingly increases late apoptotic cells (from 9.51% to 25.55%) and decreases normal living cells (from 79.47% to 57.90%), indicating copper toxicity and apoptosis induction (Fig. 4J–K, 4M, Table S7). Co-culture with COF-Bpy and Cu2+ for 24 h can reduce late apoptotic cells from 25.55% to 19.95%, confirming the promising therapeutic applications of COF-Bpy, due to its ability of alleviating copper toxicosis by adsorbing free copper (Fig. 4J, 4M-4N, Table S7).

Given the potential of COF-Bpy for copper toxicosis theranostics in cells, its efficacy in nematodes was further investigated. Firstly, fluorescence intensity decreases with the Cu2+ increasing in nematodes, displaying a linear correlation (y = −1.136x + 7.538, R2 = 0.9916), with the Cu2+ range of 0–2.0 mM (Figures 5 A-5B). Moreover, COF-Bpy can sensitively detect Cu2+ accumulation above 1 mM (P < 0.01) in nematodes (Fig. S13A).

Then, nematodes were treated with varying concentrations of COF-Bpy (100–400 μg mL−1), their survival rates in the Control, Cu group (copper-poisoned nematodes) and COF-Bpy treated groups were evaluated. The maximum lifespan of Cu group (15.0 days) is substantially shorter than that of COF-Bpy treated groups (17.3–19.0 days) (Fig. 5C–Table S8). Additionally, compared to the mean lifespan of Cu group (8.17 days), that of COF-Bpy treated groups show improvement (10.3–11.3 days), with life extension rates ranging from 26.1 to 38.3% (Fig. S13B, Table S8). Despite the lifespan improvements with COF-Bpy concentration increasing, it has sufficient therapeutic efficiency at 200 μg mL−1, which was chosen as the concentration for subsequent experiments.

Overall, these results highlight the potential of COF-Bpy for reducing free Cu2+ levels in nematodes through rapid adsorption, leading to copper toxicosis remission. Its therapeutic effect is optimal persists up to 48 h, gradually diminishing by 96 h. This study presents COF-Bpy as a promising dual-functional platform for theranostics of copper toxicosis in nematodes, providing new avenues for COF materials in vivo applications.

This study successfully developed a novel AIE COF-Bpy with a satisfactory QY of 25% through structural optimization strategy. COF-Bpy demonstrates sensitive detection of pH in the range of 2.0–14 as well as remarkable trace sensing (LOD of 3.3 pM) and good adsorption capacity (15.8 mg g−1) of Cu2+. Leveraging these properties and good biocompatibility, COF-Bpy was successfully employed as a sensitive pH probe in intracellular and an integrated platform for theranostics of copper toxicosis in cellular and nematode levels. This work offers novel insights into the development of highly emissive nanomaterials for fluorescence sensing, bioimaging and therapeutic interventions. However, pH cycling detectability of COF-Bpy and adsorption capacity of Cu2+ need to be improved. Future research should focus on exploring at the animal level (e.g. SD rat), dedicating to elucidate the specific mechanism of COF-Bpy alleviating copper toxicosis, providing new possibilities for further clinical research.

Qiuyi Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Dandan Wang: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yulian Yang: Supervision, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yunfei Zhu: Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuemeng Zou: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Mingyue Wang: Software, Investigation, Data curation. Lingling Li: Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis. Elizabeth Rosalind Thomas: Validation, Supervision. Xiang Li: Visualization, Supervision. Yuqiang Xiao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yaping Li: Methodology. Xiaogang Zhou: Supervision, Software, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Die Gao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jianming Wu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22474114), the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan (Grant Nos. 23NSFSC5092 and 2024NSFSC0610), a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2024LZXNYDJ018), a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040), a project of Intelligent Policing Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (ZNJW2024KFMS005), the Cooperation Project of Xuzhou District Government and Southwest Medical University (2021XZXNYD01), Science and Technology Planning Project of Yibin City, China [Grant Nos. 2022NY020, 2021ZYY009 and 2021ZYY005].
